why_correct: Any secret embedded in client-side code — hardcoded or injected via build-time env var — is fully recoverable by anyone who can load the page. The trust boundary breaks because the key is now outside your control perimeter: it's sitting in a public artifact that you distributed. The primary architectural failure is unbounded, unauditable API usage billed to you with no rate limiting, no user attribution, and no revocation path short of key rotation. The proxy pattern restores the trust boundary by keeping the credential server-side and letting you enforce authn/authz, rate limiting, and audit logging before any token leaves your account.

distractors.A: Key rotation without frontend redeployment is a real operational benefit of the proxy pattern, but it's a secondary convenience, not the primary reason the embedded approach is prohibited. The correct framing is that even if you could rotate instantly, the window between extraction and rotation still exposes you to credential abuse. A would be the right answer if the question were asking about operational agility rather than security requirements.
distractors.C: This is the most dangerous misconception on the team because it's partially true and sounds authoritative. Build-time env vars are indeed compiled into the bundle exactly like hardcoded strings — but the conclusion is backwards. The point isn't that one technique is safer; it's that both techniques result in a publicly readable artifact. This misconception leads engineers to believe they've secured the key when they've only obscured the source code.
distractors.D: The Anthropic API has no same-origin policy enforcing browser restrictions — it's a standard HTTPS API accessible from any origin. The latency observation about the extra network hop is real but architecturally irrelevant to the security decision.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for any credential decision: if an untrusted principal can obtain the artifact containing the secret, the secret is compromised regardless of how it was injected. Client-side === public. Any secret that must remain secret lives server-side, full stop.
cross_domain_note: This trust boundary principle reappears in agentic and tool-use architectures — when Claude has access to function-calling tools, the orchestration layer must enforce the same server-side boundary so injected tool credentials aren't leakable through prompt injection or exfiltration via model outputs.

why_correct: The system prompt is the canonical trust boundary for persistent behavioral constraints — it's evaluated on every turn and sets the behavioral envelope before any user input is processed. 'You are a helpful customer support agent' is a role declaration, not a behavioral specification; Claude infers defaults from context, and sparse prompts produce high variance outputs by design. Explicit constraints on length, tone, reading level, and structure belong in the system prompt because they are invariant across all sessions, not per-request concerns. The correct pattern when behavioral variance is causing production failures is to close the specification gap at the source, not patch downstream.

distractors.A: Prepending per-request formatting instructions works technically but creates a maintenance and trust-boundary problem: format constraints are now split across the system prompt and every user message construction path, meaning any orchestration layer change can silently drop the constraint. A is the right pattern when constraints legitimately vary per request — e.g., a multi-persona product where different user tiers get different response styles. Here, the constraint is uniform, so the architectural tell is that it belongs in the system prompt, not injected per turn.
distractors.C: The retry-loop anti-pattern looks right until you price it at scale — you're doubling worst-case latency, burning 2x token budget on every verbose response, and adding retry logic that itself needs error handling. More critically, it treats a prompt specification failure as a runtime inference problem. Claude doesn't 'learn' your length preference from a single reformatting pass; you're just adding an expensive second inference call with a slightly different prompt. In production, this breaks under any latency SLA and inflates cost per token for your highest-traffic path.
distractors.D: Fine-tuning to encode response style is a valid long-term investment for highly specialized domains, but it's the wrong tool for a formatting constraint problem. Fine-tuning is expensive, slow to iterate, and introduces a separate model lifecycle to maintain. The failure mode is over-engineering: you now own a fine-tuned model that still needs a system prompt, and any format changes require a new training run instead of a one-line prompt edit.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for this class of problem: if a behavioral property should be true for every response in a deployment, it belongs as an explicit constraint in the system prompt — never rely on role-description inference to enforce production SLAs. Sparse system prompts produce high-variance outputs; that variance is a feature for open-ended use cases and a bug for constrained UIs.
cross_domain_note: This directly connects to cost optimization: closing the specification gap at the system prompt level eliminates the retry loop's 2x token burn and latency hit, making prompt hygiene a direct lever on both cost per token and throughput capacity.

why_correct: The inconsistency originates in an underspecified system prompt — Claude is inferring format, length, and register from context, and that inference varies across query complexity. The correct pattern is to close the specification gap at the source: the system prompt is the trust boundary where behavioral contracts are established once and applied globally. Explicitly encoding max sentence count, prose-only constraint, and reading level moves format control out of the model's inference space and into deterministic instruction-following, which is the appropriate mechanism for runtime consistency.

distractors.A: Temperature controls sampling stochasticity, not output structure. Setting temperature=0 makes token selection deterministic given the same input, but different queries are different inputs — so length and markdown variance across five distinct user turns is completely unaffected. Temperature=0 is the right lever when you need identical outputs for identical inputs; it does nothing for cross-query format consistency.
distractors.C: This is the retry-loop anti-pattern — it looks pragmatic until you account for latency doubling on any response that exceeds threshold, compounded token spend on retries, and the fact that an appended mid-flight instruction still lacks the structural constraints that caused the failure. You're treating a symptom in the orchestration layer instead of fixing the root cause in the system prompt. In production this also risks cascading under load.
distractors.D: max_tokens caps truncation, not style. A 150-token ceiling will hard-cut verbose responses but won't prevent markdown, won't enforce sentence structure, and will silently truncate complex answers that legitimately need more space — degrading quality without achieving consistency.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for format and length consistency: if the variance lives across queries, the fix lives in the system prompt, not in sampling params or post-hoc retry logic. System prompt is where you establish invariants; everything else is a band-aid on a specification gap.
cross_domain_note: This pattern extends directly into agentic loop design — tool-calling agents exhibit the same drift when the system prompt underspecifies expected response structure between reasoning steps, causing downstream parsing failures in the orchestration layer.

why_correct: Streaming decouples time-to-first-token (TTFT) from total response latency — with streaming, the client receives and renders tokens progressively, so perceived wait time tracks TTFT (typically under 1s) rather than total generation time. The abandonment data tells you exactly this: users aren't rejecting long responses, they're rejecting blank screens for 8-12 seconds. Streaming solves the actual failure mode at the architecture layer, not the perception layer. The correct pattern when TTFT is your latency SLA constraint is always streaming, because it converts a synchronous block into a progressive render.

distractors.B: What makes B tempting is that it's low-effort and not wrong on its own — activity indicators do reduce abandonment versus a blank screen. But the constraint here is that users are already seeing a spinner-equivalent (blank screen with implied loading) and still abandoning at 34%. The PM's proposal addresses symptom, not cause. In production, placeholders buy you 2-4 seconds of tolerance; they don't solve an 8-12 second TTFT problem. B is the right answer in a world where you can't touch the streaming architecture; it's the wrong answer when streaming is available.
distractors.C: This is the token-budget-as-latency-fix misconception — it conflates output length with perceived responsiveness. Capping at 300 tokens degrades product quality and still delivers nothing to the user for 4-5 seconds of synchronous blocking. The failure mode in production is that you've shipped a worse product that still abandons at a meaningful rate, and you've removed architectural headroom for future use cases. Synchronous delivery under 5s is the wrong target when streaming can achieve sub-1s TTFT at any token count.
distractors.D: WebSocket eliminates HTTP handshake overhead (tens of milliseconds), but buffering the full response server-side before forwarding means the client still waits for complete generation — you've added infrastructure complexity with zero reduction in user-perceived latency. This is the transport-layer optimization applied to an application-layer problem anti-pattern.

mental_model: When abandonment data shows users leaving before output appears, the problem is TTFT, not total latency — and the only architectural fix for TTFT is streaming. UI affordances (spinners, placeholders) and token caps are mitigation strategies that operate in a different layer and leave the root cause intact. The architectural tell is the 8-12 second abandonment window correlating with blank screen time, which maps directly to buffered synchronous delivery.
cross_domain_note: This same TTFT vs. total-latency distinction applies in agentic loop design: when an orchestration layer chains multiple Claude calls, streaming intermediate results to the user during long tool-use sequences uses the same principle — progressive delivery maintains engagement across what would otherwise be opaque multi-step blocking operations.
